title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who discuss their jobs and careers. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Working Lives: Four People on Work and Career

texts.A:
### Vera, 41, Surgeon
[43]I knew I wanted to be a surgeon from the age of twelve and I pursued that goal with absolute single-mindedness.[/43] There was never a plan B. The training is extraordinarily long and demanding, and there were moments when I doubted whether I would get through it. [47]The aspect of my job I find most difficult is delivering bad news to patients and their families – no training can fully prepare you for that.[/47] [51]I work sixty to seventy hours a week on average, which has come at a real personal cost – I've missed countless important family events.[/51] I accept that, but it isn't a sacrifice I would recommend to everyone.
texts.B:
### Greg, 33, Freelance Graphic Designer
[44]I left a well-paid corporate job four years ago to work for myself and it was the best decision I ever made.[/44] Yes, the income is less predictable, but I choose my own clients, set my own hours, and work from my kitchen table. [48]The hardest part of freelancing is the isolation – there are days when I don't speak to another adult until my partner comes home in the evening.[/48] I've joined a co-working space twice a week to counter that. [52]I'd never go back to a conventional office role – the freedom is simply too valuable to give up.[/52]
texts.C:
### Joyce, 55, Secondary School Head Teacher
[45]I became a teacher because both my parents were teachers and it felt natural, but I've come to love the role entirely on its own terms.[/45] [49]Leadership is something I stepped into reluctantly – I was perfectly happy in the classroom and had no ambition to become a head.[/49] But once I was in the role, I discovered I was actually quite good at it. The most satisfying part of my job is creating the conditions for other people to do their best work. [53]The paperwork and the bureaucracy consume far too much of my time and energy, and I think that's true of almost every school leader I know.[/53]
texts.D:
### Samir, 27, Data Analyst
[46]I fell into data analysis by accident – I graduated in economics, couldn't find a relevant job, and took a short online course in data skills that changed my career direction completely.[/46] [50]I think data literacy will be the most important professional skill of the next decade and most organisations are only just beginning to realise this.[/50] I find the work genuinely absorbing. [54]The challenge is explaining what I do to non-technical colleagues and convincing them that the insights the data produces are worth acting on.[/54] People are often sceptical of numbers, especially when the numbers tell them something they don't want to hear.

questions.question: had a clear professional ambition from a very early age and pursued it without hesitation?
questions.explanation: Vera knew she wanted to be a surgeon from the age of twelve and pursued that goal with absolute single-mindedness.

questions.question: voluntarily left a more financially secure job in order to have greater independence?
questions.explanation: Greg left a well-paid corporate job to work for himself, saying it was the best decision he ever made.

questions.question: entered their profession partly through family influence but now values it for other reasons?
questions.explanation: Joyce became a teacher because both parents were teachers but has come to love the role on its own terms.

questions.question: ended up in their current career by chance rather than planning?
questions.explanation: Samir fell into data analysis by accident after a short online course redirected his career.

questions.question: finds a specific aspect of their job emotionally challenging despite their professional experience?
questions.explanation: Vera says delivering bad news to patients and families is the most difficult part of her job and no training fully prepares you for it.

questions.question: has found a practical way to deal with a particular disadvantage of working independently?
questions.explanation: Greg combats the isolation of freelancing by using a co-working space twice a week.

questions.question: admits they did not initially seek out the level of responsibility they now hold?
questions.explanation: Joyce says she stepped into leadership reluctantly and was perfectly happy as a classroom teacher without ambitions to become a head.

questions.question: predicts that a particular skill will become increasingly essential in the professional world?
questions.explanation: Samir believes data literacy will be the most important professional skill of the next decade.

questions.question: acknowledges that their career has had a negative impact on their personal and family life?
questions.explanation: Vera says her seventy-hour weeks have come at a real personal cost and she has missed countless important family events.

questions.question: states clearly that they have no intention of returning to a more conventional way of working?
questions.explanation: Greg says he would never go back to a conventional office role because the freedom is too valuable to give up.
